question:
7. Take either (i) or (ii).
(i) Show that the gravitational attraction exerted by a thin homogeneous spherical shell at an external point is the same as if the material of the shell were concentrated at its center.
(page 82)
(ii) Determine all the straight lines which lie upon the surface \( z=x y \), and draw a figure to illustrate your result.
solution:
First Solution. A thin homogeneous spherical shell is, of course, the surface of a sphere with constant uniform density, say \( \sigma \). Let the shell have radius \( a \) and let its center be the origin of a rectangular coordinate system.
Consider a particle \( P \) of mass \( m \) at the point \( (R, 0,0) \), where \( R>a \) so that Consider a particle \( P \) of mass \( m \) at the point \( (R, 0,0) \), where \( R>a \) so that
\( P \) is outside the shell. The figure shows the cross-section in the \( x-y \) plane. \( P \) is outside the shell. The figure shows the cross-section in the \( x-y \) p
We shall calculate the gravitational attraction between \( P \) and the shell.

Because of rotational symmetry the resultant force is directed along the \( x \)-axis; hence we need compute only the \( x \)-component of the force. The short arc of length \( a \Delta \theta \) shown in the figure generates (on rotation about mass \( 2 \pi a^{2} \sigma \sin \theta \Delta \theta \). Let \( r \) and \( \phi \) be as shown in the figure. Then every mass \( 2 \pi a^{2} \sigma \sin \theta \Delta \theta \). Let \( r \) and \( \phi \) be as shown in the figure. Then every
point of \( Z \) is (essentially) at distance \( r \) from \( P \) and acts along a line making angle \( \phi \) with the \( x \)-axis. Hence the \( x \)-component of the gravitational attraction between \( Z \) and \( P \) has the approximate magnitude
\[
\frac{G m \cdot 2 \pi a^{2} \sigma \sin \theta \Delta \theta}{r^{2}} \cos \phi
\]
where \( G \) is the constant of gravitation. Therefore the entire attractive force between the shell and \( P \) is
\[
\boldsymbol{F}=2 \pi G m a^{2} \sigma \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{\cos \phi}{r^{2}} \sin \theta d \theta
\]

Here \( \phi \) and \( r \) are functions of \( \theta \).
We have \( r \cos \phi+a \cos \theta=R \), so the integral becomes
\[
2 \pi G m a^{2} \sigma \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{R-a \cos \theta}{r^{3}} \sin \theta d \theta
\]

This apparently complicated integral can be easily evaluated if we change the variable of integration to \( r \). By the law of cosines we have
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\qquad r^{2}=R^{2}+a^{2}-2 a R \cos \theta . \\
\text { Therefore } \\
\qquad R-a \cos \theta=\frac{1}{2 R}\left(R^{2}-a^{2}+r^{2}\right) \\
\text { and } r d r=a R \sin \theta d \theta \text {. Hence } \\
\text { (1) } \begin{aligned}
\\
\qquad \begin{aligned}
F & =\frac{\pi G m a \sigma}{R^{2}} \int_{R-a}^{R+a} \frac{R^{2}-a^{2}+r^{2}}{r^{2}} d r \\
= & \frac{\pi G m a \sigma}{R^{2}}\left[\left(R^{2}-a^{2}\right)\left[\frac{1}{R-a}-\frac{1}{R+a}\right]+2 a\right] \\
= & \frac{4 \pi G m a^{2} \sigma}{R^{2}}=\frac{G m M}{R^{2}}
\end{aligned}
\end{aligned} .
\end{array}
\]
where \( M=4 \pi a^{2} \sigma \) is the mass of the spherical shell. Thus the force is the same as it would be if all the mass of the shell were concentrated at the center.

Remark 1. It follows from this result that any spherically homogeneous mass distribution within a sphere \( S \) has the same gravitational field at points outside \( S \) as it would have if all the mass were concentrated at the center of \( S \).
Remark 2. The same computations handle the case in which \( P \) is within the shell except that \( R<a \) and the limits of integration in (1) are from \( a-R \) to \( a+R \). We find then \( F=0 \). Thus the attraction of a homogeneous spherical shell on a particle inside it is always zero.
Second Solution. Gauss's theorem states that if a closed surface \( S \) contains a mass \( M \) in its interior, and if \( \vec{F} \) is the gravitational field due to the mass, then the total flux through \( S \) is
\[
\int_{S} \int \vec{F} \cdot \vec{n} d A=-4 G \pi M
\]
where \( \vec{n} \) is the outward-drawn unit normal to \( S \) and \( d A \) is the element of surface area.
Now let \( S \) be a sphere of radius \( R \) external to the given shell and concentric with it. By symmetry,
\[
\vec{F}=-F(R) \vec{n} \text { on } S
\]
where \( F=F(R) \) is a constant on \( S \). Thus,
\[
-4 G \pi M=\int_{S} \int-F(R) \vec{n} \cdot \vec{n} d A=-F(R) \int_{S} \int d A=-4 \pi R^{2} F(R)
\]
whence \( F(R)=G M / R^{2} \), and as a vector \( \vec{F}=-\left(G M / R^{2}\right) \vec{n} \).
Solution. Suppose that \( L \) is a line through \( \left(x_{0}, y_{0}, z_{0}\right) \) which lies in the surface \( z=x y \). \( L \) can be represented parametrically by
\[
\begin{array}{l}
x=x_{0}+\alpha t \\
y=y_{0}+\beta t \\
z=z_{0}+\gamma t
\end{array}
\]
where \( \alpha, \beta, \gamma \) are not all zero. In order that \( L \) lie in the given surface it is necessary and sufficient that
\[
z_{0}+\gamma t=\left(x_{0}+\alpha t\right)\left(y_{0}+\beta t\right)=x_{0} y_{0}+\left(\alpha y_{0}+\beta x_{0}\right) t+\alpha \beta t^{2}
\]
for all values of \( t \). This implies that \( \alpha \beta \), the coefficient of \( t^{2} \), is zero; hence either \( \alpha=0 \) or \( \beta=0 \).
If \( \alpha=0 \), then \( \gamma=\beta x_{0} \), while if \( \beta=0 \), then \( \gamma=\alpha y_{0} \). We cannot have both \( \alpha \) and \( \beta \) zero, since that would imply also \( \gamma=0 \). Hence we can normalize whichever of these is non-zero to be one. Therefore the parametric equations of \( L \) can be put in one of the two forms
\[
\begin{array}{lll}
x=x_{0} & x & =x_{0}+t \\
y=y_{0}+t & \text { or } & y=y_{0} \\
z=z_{0}+x_{0} t & & z=z_{0}+y_{0} t
\end{array}
\]
with corresponding non-parametric forms
\[
\begin{array}{lll}
x=x_{0} & \text { or } & y=y_{0} \\
z=x_{0} y & & z=y_{0} x .
\end{array}
\]

Conversely, it is obvious that any such line lies entirely on the surface.
An alternative procedure depends on the following fact: If a line \( L \) through a point \( P \) lies entirely on a surface \( S \), then \( L \) lies in the tangent plane to \( S \) at \( P \).
For the given surface
(1)
\[
z=x y
\]
the tangent plane at \( \left(x_{0}, y_{0}, z_{0}\right) \) is
(2)
\[
\left(x-x_{0}\right) y_{0}+\left(y-y_{0}\right) x_{0}+\left(z-z_{0}\right)(-1)=0 .
\]

Eliminating \( z \) between the last two equations we find
(3)
\[
\left(x-x_{0}\right)\left(y-y_{0}\right)=0
\]
and this is the equation of the projection on the \( x y \)-plane of the intersection of the surface (1) with its tangent plane (2). The projection consists of two lines
and these come from two lines
```
x=\mp@subsup{x}{0}{}\quad\mathrm{ and }\quady=\mp@subsup{y}{0}{}
z=\mp@subsup{x}{0}{}y\quadz
```
lying on (1) and (2).

descriptive_long.question:
7. Take either (i) or (ii).
(i) Show that the gravitational attraction exerted by a thin homogeneous spherical shell at an external point is the same as if the material of the shell were concentrated at its center.
(page 82)
(ii) Determine all the straight lines which lie upon the surface \( zcoordinate = xcoordinate ycoordinate \), and draw a figure to illustrate your result.
descriptive_long.solution:
First Solution. A thin homogeneous spherical shell is, of course, the surface of a sphere with constant uniform density, say \( surfacedensity \). Let the shell have radius \( radiusshell \) and let its center be the origin of a rectangular coordinate system.
Consider a particle \( pointpee \) of mass \( masspoint \) at the point \( (radiusbig, 0,0) \), where \( radiusbig>radiusshell \) so that
\( pointpee \) is outside the shell. The figure shows the cross-section in the \( xcoordinate\! -\! ycoordinate \) plane.
We shall calculate the gravitational attraction between \( pointpee \) and the shell.

Because of rotational symmetry the resultant force is directed along the \( xcoordinate \)-axis; hence we need compute only the \( xcoordinate \)-component of the force. The short arc of length \( radiusshell\,\Delta thetavar \) shown in the figure generates (on rotation about mass \( 2\pi radiusshell^{2} surfacedensity \sin thetavar\,\Delta thetavar \). Let \( rscalar \) and \( phivar \) be as shown in the figure. Then every
point of \( arczed \) is (essentially) at distance \( rscalar \) from \( pointpee \) and acts along a line making angle \( phivar \) with the \( xcoordinate \)-axis. Hence the \( xcoordinate \)-component of the gravitational attraction between \( arczed \) and \( pointpee \) has the approximate magnitude
\[
\frac{gravconst\, masspoint \cdot 2 \pi radiusshell^{2} surfacedensity \sin thetavar \,\Delta thetavar}{rscalar^{2}} \cos phivar
\]
where \( gravconst \) is the constant of gravitation. Therefore the entire attractive force between the shell and \( pointpee \) is
\[
\mathbf{forcevec}=2 \pi gravconst \; masspoint \; radiusshell^{2} surfacedensity \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{\cos phivar}{rscalar^{2}} \sin thetavar\, d thetavar
\]

Here \( phivar \) and \( rscalar \) are functions of \( thetavar \).
We have \( rscalar \cos phivar + radiusshell \cos thetavar = radiusbig \), so the integral becomes
\[
2 \pi gravconst \, masspoint \, radiusshell^{2} surfacedensity \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{radiusbig-radiusshell \cos thetavar}{rscalar^{3}} \sin thetavar \, d thetavar
\]

This apparently complicated integral can be easily evaluated if we change the variable of integration to \( rscalar \). By the law of cosines we have
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\qquad rscalar^{2}=radiusbig^{2}+radiusshell^{2}-2\, radiusshell\, radiusbig \cos thetavar . \\
\text { Therefore } \\
\qquad radiusbig-radiusshell \cos thetavar=\frac{1}{2 radiusbig}\left(radiusbig^{2}-radiusshell^{2}+rscalar^{2}\right) \\
\text { and } rscalar \, d rscalar = radiusshell \, radiusbig \sin thetavar \, d thetavar .\end{array}
\]
Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
forcevec &= \frac{\pi \, gravconst \; masspoint \, radiusshell \, surfacedensity}{radiusbig^{2}} \int_{radiusbig-radiusshell}^{radiusbig+radiusshell} \frac{radiusbig^{2}-radiusshell^{2}+rscalar^{2}}{rscalar^{2}} \, d rscalar \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\pi \, gravconst \; masspoint \, radiusshell \, surfacedensity}{radiusbig^{2}} \Big[\big(radiusbig^{2}-radiusshell^{2}\big)\Big(\frac{1}{radiusbig-radiusshell}-\frac{1}{radiusbig+radiusshell}\Big)+2 radiusshell\Big] \\[4pt]
&= \frac{4 \pi \, gravconst \, masspoint \, radiusshell^{2} surfacedensity}{radiusbig^{2}} = \frac{gravconst \, masspoint \, shellmass}{radiusbig^{2}}
\end{aligned}
\]
where \( shellmass = 4 \pi radiusshell^{2} surfacedensity \) is the mass of the spherical shell. Thus the force is the same as it would be if all the mass of the shell were concentrated at the center.

Remark 1. It follows from this result that any spherically homogeneous mass distribution within a sphere \( surface \) has the same gravitational field at points outside \( surface \) as it would have if all the mass were concentrated at the center of \( surface \).
Remark 2. The same computations handle the case in which \( pointpee \) is within the shell except that \( radiusbig<radiusshell \) and the limits of integration are from \( radiusshell-radiusbig \) to \( radiusshell+radiusbig \). We find then \( forcevec=0 \). Thus the attraction of a homogeneous spherical shell on a particle inside it is always zero.

Second Solution. Gauss's theorem states that if a closed \( surface \) contains a mass \( shellmass \) in its interior, and if \( \vec{forcevec} \) is the gravitational field due to the mass, then the total flux through \( surface \) is
\[
\int_{surface} \!\! \int \vec{forcevec} \cdot \vec{normalvec} \, d areaelem = -4 \pi \, gravconst \, shellmass
\]
where \( \vec{normalvec} \) is the outward-drawn unit normal to \( surface \) and \( d areaelem \) is the element of surface area.
Now let \( surface \) be a sphere of radius \( radiusbig \) external to the given shell and concentric with it. By symmetry,
\[
\vec{forcevec}= -F(radiusbig)\, \vec{normalvec} \text{ on } surface
\]
where \( F(radiusbig) \) is a constant on \( surface \). Thus,
\[
-4 \pi \, gravconst \, shellmass = \int_{surface} \!\! \int -F(radiusbig) \vec{normalvec} \cdot \vec{normalvec} \, d areaelem = -F(radiusbig) \int_{surface} \!\! \int d areaelem = -4 \pi radiusbig^{2} F(radiusbig)
\]
whence \( F(radiusbig)=gravconst \, shellmass / radiusbig^{2} \), and as a vector \( \vec{forcevec}= -\big(gravconst \, shellmass / radiusbig^{2}\big) \vec{normalvec} \).

Solution. Suppose that \( lineell \) is a line through \( (xbasept, ybasept, zbasept) \) which lies in the surface \( zcoordinate = xcoordinate ycoordinate \). \( lineell \) can be represented parametrically by
\[
\begin{array}{l}
 xcoordinate = xbasept + alphaparam \, parameter \\
 ycoordinate = ybasept + betaparam \, parameter \\
 zcoordinate = zbasept + gammaparam \, parameter
\end{array}
\]
where \( alphaparam, betaparam, gammaparam \) are not all zero. In order that \( lineell \) lie in the given surface it is necessary and sufficient that
\[
 zbasept + gammaparam \, parameter = (xbasept + alphaparam \, parameter)(ybasept + betaparam \, parameter) = xbasept ybasept + (alphaparam ybasept + betaparam xbasept) \, parameter + alphaparam betaparam \, parameter^{2}
\]
for all values of \( parameter \). This implies that \( alphaparam betaparam \), the coefficient of \( parameter^{2} \), is zero; hence either \( alphaparam = 0 \) or \( betaparam = 0 \).
If \( alphaparam = 0 \), then \( gammaparam = betaparam xbasept \), while if \( betaparam = 0 \), then \( gammaparam = alphaparam ybasept \). We cannot have both \( alphaparam \) and \( betaparam \) zero, since that would imply also \( gammaparam=0 \). Hence we can normalize whichever of these is non-zero to be one. Therefore the parametric equations of \( lineell \) can be put in one of the two forms
\[
\begin{array}{lll}
 xcoordinate = xbasept & xcoordinate & = xbasept + parameter \\
 ycoordinate = ybasept + parameter & \text{ or } & ycoordinate = ybasept \\
 zcoordinate = zbasept + xbasept \, parameter & & zcoordinate = zbasept + ybasept \, parameter
\end{array}
\]
with corresponding non-parametric forms
\[
\begin{array}{lll}
 xcoordinate = xbasept & \text{ or } & ycoordinate = ybasept \\
 zcoordinate = xbasept \, ycoordinate & & zcoordinate = ybasept \, xcoordinate .
\end{array}
\]

Conversely, it is obvious that any such line lies entirely on the surface.

An alternative procedure depends on the following fact: If a line \( lineell \) through a point \( pointpee \) lies entirely on a surface \( surface \), then \( lineell \) lies in the tangent plane to \( surface \) at \( pointpee \).
For the given surface
(1)
\[
 zcoordinate = xcoordinate ycoordinate
\]
the tangent plane at \( (xbasept, ybasept, zbasept) \) is
(2)
\[
 (xcoordinate - xbasept) ybasept + (ycoordinate - ybasept) xbasept + (zcoordinate - zbasept)(-1)=0 .
\]

Eliminating \( zcoordinate \) between the last two equations we find
(3)
\[
 (xcoordinate - xbasept)(ycoordinate - ybasept)=0
\]
and this is the equation of the projection on the \( xcoordinate ycoordinate \)-plane of the intersection of the surface (1) with its tangent plane (2). The projection consists of two lines and these come from two lines
\[
 xcoordinate = xbasept \quad \text{and} \quad ycoordinate = ybasept \\
 zcoordinate = xbasept ycoordinate \quad \text{or} \quad zcoordinate = ybasept xcoordinate
\]
lying on (1) and (2).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
7. Take either (i) or (ii).
(i) Show that the gravitational attraction exerted by a thin homogeneous spherical shell at an external point is the same as if the material of the shell were concentrated at its center.
(page 82)
(ii) Determine all the straight lines which lie upon the surface \( lighthouse = meadowland\,copperleaf \), and draw a figure to illustrate your result.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
First Solution. A thin homogeneous spherical shell is, of course, the surface of a sphere with constant uniform density, say \( ironsong \). Let the shell have radius \( stoneveil \) and let its center be the origin of a rectangular coordinate system.
Consider a particle \( starflower \) of mass \( thundersap \) at the point \( (riverstone,0,0) \), where \( riverstone>stoneveil \) so that \( starflower \) is outside the shell. The figure shows the cross-section in the \( meadowland
dots copperleaf \) plane. We shall calculate the gravitational attraction between \( starflower \) and the shell.

Because of rotational symmetry the resultant force is directed along the \( meadowland \)-axis; hence we need compute only the \( meadowland \)-component of the force. The short arc of length \( stoneveil\,\Delta rainshard \) shown in the figure generates (on rotation about the axis) a mass \( 2\pi stoneveil^{2} ironsong\sin rainshard\,\Delta rainshard \). Let \( moonstone \) and \( shadowfen \) be as shown in the figure. Then every point of \( emberglow \) is (essentially) at distance \( moonstone \) from \( starflower \) and acts along a line making angle \( shadowfen \) with the \( meadowland \)-axis. Hence the \( meadowland \)-component of the gravitational attraction between \( emberglow \) and \( starflower \) has the approximate magnitude
\[
\frac{quillfire\,thundersap\;2\pi stoneveil^{2} ironsong\sin rainshard\,\Delta rainshard}{moonstone^{2}}\cos shadowfen
\]
where \( quillfire \) is the constant of gravitation. Therefore the entire attractive force between the shell and \( starflower \) is
\[
\boldsymbol{frostwind}=2\pi quillfire\,thundersap\,stoneveil^{2} ironsong\int_{0}^{\pi}\frac{\cos shadowfen}{moonstone^{2}}\sin rainshard\,d rainshard.
\]
Here \( shadowfen \) and \( moonstone \) are functions of \( rainshard \).
We have \( moonstone\cos shadowfen+stoneveil\cos rainshard=riverstone \), so the integral becomes
\[
2\pi quillfire\,thundersap\,stoneveil^{2} ironsong\int_{0}^{\pi}\frac{riverstone-stoneveil\cos rainshard}{moonstone^{3}}\sin rainshard\,d rainshard.
\]
This apparently complicated integral can be easily evaluated if we change the variable of integration to \( moonstone \). By the law of cosines we have
\[
moonstone^{2}=riverstone^{2}+stoneveil^{2}-2 stoneveil\,riverstone\cos rainshard .
\]
Therefore
\[
riverstone-stoneveil\cos rainshard=\frac{1}{2 riverstone}\bigl(riverstone^{2}-stoneveil^{2}+moonstone^{2}\bigr), \qquad moonstone\,d moonstone=stoneveil\,riverstone\sin rainshard\,d rainshard.
\]
Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
frostwind&=\frac{\pi quillfire\,thundersap\,stoneveil\,ironsong}{riverstone^{2}}
\int_{riverstone-stoneveil}^{riverstone+stoneveil}\frac{riverstone^{2}-stoneveil^{2}+moonstone^{2}}{moonstone^{2}}\,d moonstone\\
&=\frac{\pi quillfire\,thundersap\,stoneveil\,ironsong}{riverstone^{2}}
\left[(riverstone^{2}-stoneveil^{2})\Bigl(\frac{1}{riverstone-stoneveil}-\frac{1}{riverstone+stoneveil}\Bigr)+2 stoneveil\right]\\
&=\frac{4\pi quillfire\,thundersap\,stoneveil^{2} ironsong}{riverstone^{2}}
=\frac{quillfire\,thundersap\,glenforge}{riverstone^{2}},
\end{aligned}
\]
where \( glenforge = 4\pi stoneveil^{2} ironsong \) is the mass of the spherical shell. Thus the force is the same as it would be if all the mass of the shell were concentrated at the center.

Remark 1. It follows from this result that any spherically homogeneous mass distribution within a sphere \( duskpetal \) has the same gravitational field at points outside \( duskpetal \) as it would have if all the mass were concentrated at the center of \( duskpetal \).

Remark 2. The same computations handle the case in which \( starflower \) is within the shell except that \( riverstone<stoneveil \) and the limits of integration above are from \( stoneveil-riverstone \) to \( stoneveil+riverstone \). We find then \( frostwind=0 \). Thus the attraction of a homogeneous spherical shell on a particle inside it is always zero.

Second Solution. Gauss's theorem states that if a closed surface \( duskpetal \) contains a mass \( glenforge \) in its interior, and if \( \vec{frostwind} \) is the gravitational field due to the mass, then the total flux through \( duskpetal \) is
\[
\int_{duskpetal}\!\int \vec{frostwind}\cdot\vec{cloudroot}\,d sagebloom = -4\pi quillfire\,glenforge,
\]
where \( \vec{cloudroot} \) is the outward-drawn unit normal to \( duskpetal \) and \( d sagebloom \) is the element of surface area.
Now let \( duskpetal \) be a sphere of radius \( riverstone \) external to the given shell and concentric with it. By symmetry,
\[
\vec{frostwind}=-frostwind(riverstone)\,\vec{cloudroot}\quad\text{on }duskpetal,
\]
where \( frostwind(riverstone) \) is constant on \( duskpetal \). Thus,
\[
-4\pi quillfire\,glenforge = \int_{duskpetal}\!\int -frostwind(riverstone)\,\vec{cloudroot}\cdot\vec{cloudroot}\,d sagebloom = -frostwind(riverstone)\, \int_{duskpetal}\!\int d sagebloom = -4\pi riverstone^{2} frostwind(riverstone),
\]
whence \( frostwind(riverstone)=quillfire\,glenforge/riverstone^{2} \), and as a vector \( \vec{frostwind} = -(quillfire\,glenforge/riverstone^{2})\,\vec{cloudroot} \).

Solution. Suppose that \( silktrail \) is a line through \( (meadowseed,\,copperseed,\,lightseed) \) which lies in the surface \( lighthouse = meadowland\,copperleaf \). \( silktrail \) can be represented parametrically by
\[
\begin{aligned}
meadowland &= meadowseed + whisperer\,grainflow,\\
copperleaf &= copperseed + snowdrift\,grainflow,\\
lighthouse &= lightseed + amberwing\,grainflow,
\end{aligned}
\]
where \( whisperer, snowdrift, amberwing \) are not all zero. In order that \( silktrail \) lie in the given surface it is necessary and sufficient that
\[
lightseed + amberwing\,grainflow = (meadowseed + whisperer\,grainflow)(copperseed + snowdrift\,grainflow) = meadowseed\,copperseed + (whisperer\,copperseed + snowdrift\,meadowseed)\,grainflow + whisperer\,snowdrift\,grainflow^{2}
\]
for all values of \( grainflow \). This implies that \( whisperer\,snowdrift = 0 \); hence either \( whisperer=0 \) or \( snowdrift=0 \).

If \( whisperer=0 \), then \( amberwing = snowdrift\,meadowseed \), while if \( snowdrift=0 \), then \( amberwing = whisperer\,copperseed \). We cannot have both \( whisperer \) and \( snowdrift \) zero, since that would imply also \( amberwing=0 \). Normalizing the non-zero one to 1, the parametric equations of \( silktrail \) can be put in one of the two forms
\[
\begin{array}{lll}
meadowland = meadowseed, & & meadowland = meadowseed + grainflow,\\
copperleaf = copperseed + grainflow, & \text{or} & copperleaf = copperseed,\\
lighthouse = lightseed + meadowseed\,grainflow, & & lighthouse = lightseed + copperseed\,grainflow.
\end{array}
\]
with corresponding non-parametric forms
\[
\begin{array}{lll}
meadowland = meadowseed, & \text{or} & copperleaf = copperseed,\\
lighthouse = meadowseed\,copperleaf, & & lighthouse = copperseed\,meadowland.
\end{array}
\]
Conversely, it is obvious that any such line lies entirely on the surface.

An alternative procedure depends on the following fact: If a line \( silktrail \) through a point \( P \) lies entirely on a surface \( S \), then \( silktrail \) lies in the tangent plane to \( S \) at \( P \).

For the given surface
\[
(1)\qquad lighthouse = meadowland\,copperleaf,
\]

the tangent plane at \( (meadowseed,\,copperseed,\,lightseed) \) is
\[
(2)\qquad (meadowland - meadowseed)\,copperseed + (copperleaf - copperseed)\,meadowseed - (lighthouse - lightseed) = 0.
\]
Eliminating \( lighthouse \) between the last two equations we find
\[
(3)\qquad (meadowland - meadowseed)(copperleaf - copperseed) = 0,
\]
and this is the equation of the projection on the \( meadowland\,copperleaf \)-plane of the intersection of the surface (1) with its tangent plane (2). The projection consists of two lines, and these come from two lines
\[
meadowland = meadowseed \quad\text{and}\quad copperleaf = copperseed,
\]
with
\[
lighthouse = meadowseed\,copperleaf \quad\text{and}\quad lighthouse = copperseed\,meadowland,
\]
lying on (1) and (2).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
7. Take either (i) or (ii).
(i) Show that the gravitational attraction exerted by a thin homogeneous spherical shell at an external point is the same as if the material of the shell were concentrated at its center.
(page 82)
(ii) Determine all the straight lines which lie upon the surface \( planeflat=verticalaxis horizontalaxis \), and draw a figure to illustrate your result.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
First Solution. A thin homogeneous spherical shell is, of course, the surface of a sphere with constant uniform density, say \( vacuumness \). Let the shell have radius \( thickness \) and let its center be the origin of a rectangular coordinate system.
Consider a particle \( voidpoint \) of mass \( emptiness \) at the point \( (intimacy, 0,0) \), where \( intimacy>thickness \) so that Consider a particle \( voidpoint \) of mass \( emptiness \) at the point \( (intimacy, 0,0) \), where \( intimacy>thickness \) so that
\( voidpoint \) is outside the shell. The figure shows the cross-section in the \( verticalaxis-horizontalaxis \) plane. \( voidpoint \) is outside the shell. The figure shows the cross-section in the \( verticalaxis-horizontalaxis \) p
We shall calculate the gravitational attraction between \( voidpoint \) and the shell.

Because of rotational symmetry the resultant force is directed along the \( verticalaxis \)-axis; hence we need compute only the \( verticalaxis \)-component of the force. The short arc of length \( thickness\,\Delta straightangle \) shown in the figure generates (on rotation about mass \( 2 \pi thickness^{2} vacuumness \sin straightangle \Delta straightangle \). Let \( closeness \) and \( nullangle \) be as shown in the figure. Then every mass \( 2 \pi thickness^{2} vacuumness \sin straightangle \Delta straightangle \). Let \( closeness \) and \( nullangle \) be as shown in the figure. Then every
point of \( wholemass \) is (essentially) at distance \( closeness \) from \( voidpoint \) and acts along a line making angle \( nullangle \) with the \( verticalaxis \)-axis. Hence the \( verticalaxis \)-component of the gravitational attraction between \( wholemass \) and \( voidpoint \) has the approximate magnitude
\[
\frac{repulsionc\ emptiness \cdot 2 \pi thickness^{2} vacuumness \sin straightangle \Delta straightangle}{closeness^{2}} \cos nullangle
\]
where \( repulsionc \) is the constant of gravitation. Therefore the entire attractive force between the shell and \( voidpoint \) is
\[
\boldsymbol{calmness}=2 \pi repulsionc\ emptiness\ thickness^{2} vacuumness \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{\cos nullangle}{closeness^{2}} \sin straightangle d straightangle
\]

Here \( nullangle \) and \( closeness \) are functions of \( straightangle \).
We have \( closeness \cos nullangle+thickness \cos straightangle=intimacy \), so the integral becomes
\[
2 \pi repulsionc\ emptiness\ thickness^{2} vacuumness \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{intimacy-thickness \cos straightangle}{closeness^{3}} \sin straightangle d straightangle
\]

This apparently complicated integral can be easily evaluated if we change the variable of integration to \( closeness \). By the law of cosines we have
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\qquad closeness^{2}=intimacy^{2}+thickness^{2}-2\ thickness\ intimacy \cos straightangle . \\
\text { Therefore } \\
\qquad intimacy-thickness \cos straightangle=\frac{1}{2 intimacy}\left(intimacy^{2}-thickness^{2}+closeness^{2}\right) \\
\text { and } closeness\ d closeness=thickness\ intimacy \sin straightangle d straightangle \text {. Hence } \\
\text { (1) } \begin{aligned}
\\
\qquad \begin{aligned}
calmness & =\frac{\pi repulsionc\ emptiness\ thickness\ vacuumness}{intimacy^{2}} \int_{intimacy-thickness}^{intimacy+thickness} \frac{intimacy^{2}-thickness^{2}+closeness^{2}}{closeness^{2}} d closeness \\
= & \frac{\pi repulsionc\ emptiness\ thickness\ vacuumness}{intimacy^{2}}\left[\left(intimacy^{2}-thickness^{2}\right)\left[\frac{1}{intimacy-thickness}-\frac{1}{intimacy+thickness}\right]+2\ thickness\right] \\
= & \frac{4 \pi repulsionc\ emptiness\ thickness^{2} vacuumness}{intimacy^{2}}=\frac{repulsionc\ emptiness\ masslessness}{intimacy^{2}}
\end{aligned}
\end{aligned} .
\end{array}
\]
where \( masslessness=4 \pi thickness^{2} vacuumness \) is the mass of the spherical shell. Thus the force is the same as it would be if all the mass of the shell were concentrated at the center.

Remark 1. It follows from this result that any spherically homogeneous mass distribution within a sphere \( straightline \) has the same gravitational field at points outside \( straightline \) as it would have if all the mass were concentrated at the center of \( straightline \).
Remark 2. The same computations handle the case in which \( voidpoint \) is within the shell except that \( intimacy<thickness \) and the limits of integration in (1) are from \( thickness-intimacy \) to \( thickness+intimacy \). We find then \( calmness=0 \). Thus the attraction of a homogeneous spherical shell on a particle inside it is always zero.
Second Solution. Gauss's theorem states that if a closed surface \( straightline \) contains a mass \( masslessness \) in its interior, and if \( \vec{calmness} \) is the gravitational field due to the mass, then the total flux through \( straightline \) is
\[
\int_{straightline} \int \vec{calmness} \cdot \vec{parallelvector} d volumeelem=-4 repulsionc \pi masslessness
\]
where \( \vec{parallelvector} \) is the outward-drawn unit normal to \( straightline \) and \( d volumeelem \) is the element of surface area.
Now let \( straightline \) be a sphere of radius \( intimacy \) external to the given shell and concentric with it. By symmetry,
\[
\vec{calmness}=-calmness(intimacy) \vec{parallelvector} \text { on } straightline
\]
where \( calmness=calmness(intimacy) \) is a constant on \( straightline \). Thus,
\[
-4 repulsionc \pi masslessness=\int_{straightline} \int-calmness(intimacy) \vec{parallelvector} \cdot \vec{parallelvector} d volumeelem=-calmness(intimacy) \int_{straightline} \int d volumeelem=-4 \pi intimacy^{2} calmness(intimacy)
\]
whence \( calmness(intimacy)=repulsionc\ masslessness / intimacy^{2} \), and as a vector \( \vec{calmness}=-(repulsionc\ masslessness / intimacy^{2}) \vec{parallelvector} \).
Solution. Suppose that \( curvepath \) is a line through \( (finalvertical, finalhorizontal, finalplanar) \) which lies in the surface \( planeflat=verticalaxis horizontalaxis \). \( curvepath \) can be represented parametrically by
\[
\begin{array}{l}
verticalaxis=finalvertical+omegaindex stillness \\
horizontalaxis=finalhorizontal+endindex stillness \\
planeflat=finalplanar+voidvalue stillness
\end{array}
\]
where \( omegaindex, endindex, voidvalue \) are not all zero. In order that \( curvepath \) lie in the given surface it is necessary and sufficient that
\[
finalplanar+voidvalue stillness=(finalvertical+omegaindex stillness)(finalhorizontal+endindex stillness)=finalvertical finalhorizontal+(omegaindex finalhorizontal+endindex finalvertical) stillness+omegaindex endindex stillness^{2}
\]
for all values of \( stillness \). This implies that \( omegaindex endindex \), the coefficient of \( stillness^{2} \), is zero; hence either \( omegaindex=0 \) or \( endindex=0 \).
If \( omegaindex=0 \), then \( voidvalue=endindex finalvertical \), while if \( endindex=0 \), then \( voidvalue=omegaindex finalhorizontal \). We cannot have both \( omegaindex \) and \( endindex \) zero, since that would imply also \( voidvalue=0 \). Hence we can normalize whichever of these is non-zero to be one. Therefore the parametric equations of \( curvepath \) can be put in one of the two forms
\[
\begin{array}{lll}
verticalaxis=finalvertical & verticalaxis & =finalvertical+stillness \\
horizontalaxis=finalhorizontal+stillness & \text { or } & horizontalaxis=finalhorizontal \\
planeflat=finalplanar+finalvertical stillness & & planeflat=finalplanar+finalhorizontal stillness
\end{array}
\]
with corresponding non-parametric forms
\[
\begin{array}{lll}
verticalaxis=finalvertical & \text { or } & horizontalaxis=finalhorizontal \\
planeflat=finalvertical horizontalaxis & & planeflat=finalhorizontal verticalaxis .
\end{array}
\]

Conversely, it is obvious that any such line lies entirely on the surface.
An alternative procedure depends on the following fact: If a line \( curvepath \) through a point \( voidpoint \) lies entirely on a surface \( straightline \), then \( curvepath \) lies in the tangent plane to \( straightline \) at \( voidpoint \).
For the given surface
(1)
\[
planeflat=verticalaxis horizontalaxis
\]
the tangent plane at \( (finalvertical, finalhorizontal, finalplanar) \) is
(2)
\[
(verticalaxis-finalvertical) finalhorizontal+(horizontalaxis-finalhorizontal) finalvertical+(planeflat-finalplanar)(-1)=0 .
\]

Eliminating \( planeflat \) between the last two equations we find
(3)
\[
(verticalaxis-finalvertical)(horizontalaxis-finalhorizontal)=0
\]
and this is the equation of the projection on the \( verticalaxis horizontalaxis \)-plane of the intersection of the surface (1) with its tangent plane (2). The projection consists of two lines
and these come from two lines
```
verticalaxis=\mp@subsup{finalvertical}{}\quad\mathrm{ and }\quad horizontalaxis=\mp@subsup{finalhorizontal}{}
planeflat=\mp@subsup{finalvertical}{}horizontalaxis\quad planeflat
```
lying on (1) and (2).

garbled_string.question:
7. Take either (i) or (ii).
(i) Show that the gravitational attraction exerted by a thin homogeneous spherical shell at an external point is the same as if the material of the shell were concentrated at its center.
(page 82)
(ii) Determine all the straight lines which lie upon the surface \( vbedmciu=qzxwvtnp hjgrksla \), and draw a figure to illustrate your result.
garbled_string.solution:
First Solution. A thin homogeneous spherical shell is, of course, the surface of a sphere with constant uniform density, say \( ycvrmpth \). Let the shell have radius \( fjwsnoxa \) and let its center be the origin of a rectangular coordinate system.
Consider a particle \( wsykrdjl \) of mass \( oaxfbrge \) at the point \( (ekswptzy,0,0) \), where \( ekswptzy>fjwsnoxa \) so that Consider a particle \( wsykrdjl \) of mass \( oaxfbrge \) at the point \( (ekswptzy,0,0) \), where \( ekswptzy>fjwsnoxa \) so that
\( wsykrdjl \) is outside the shell. The figure shows the cross-section in the \( qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla \) plane. \( wsykrdjl \) is outside the shell. The figure shows the cross-section in the \( qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla \) p
We shall calculate the gravitational attraction between \( wsykrdjl \) and the shell.

Because of rotational symmetry the resultant force is directed along the \( qzxwvtnp \)-axis; hence we need compute only the \( qzxwvtnp \)-component of the force. The short arc of length \( fjwsnoxa \Delta mjztkure \) shown in the figure generates (on rotation about mass \( 2\pi fjwsnoxa^{2} ycvrmpth\sin mjztkure \Delta mjztkure \). Let \( psnrlvqa \) and \( lxvceqgd \) be as shown in the figure. Then every mass \( 2\pi fjwsnoxa^{2} ycvrmpth\sin mjztkure \Delta mjztkure \). Let \( psnrlvqa \) and \( lxvceqgd \) be as shown in the figure. Then every
point of \( ktpxwodg \) is (essentially) at distance \( psnrlvqa \) from \( wsykrdjl \) and acts along a line making angle \( lxvceqgd \) with the \( qzxwvtnp \)-axis. Hence the \( qzxwvtnp \)-component of the gravitational attraction between \( ktpxwodg \) and \( wsykrdjl \) has the approximate magnitude
\[
\frac{knsqbalv\,oaxfbrge\cdot2\pi fjwsnoxa^{2} ycvrmpth\sin mjztkure \Delta mjztkure}{psnrlvqa^{2}}\cos lxvceqgd
\]
where \( knsqbalv \) is the constant of gravitation. Therefore the entire attractive force between the shell and \( wsykrdjl \) is
\[
\boldsymbol{dsqjplui}=2\pi knsqbalv\,oaxfbrge\,fjwsnoxa^{2} ycvrmpth\int_{0}^{\pi}\frac{\cos lxvceqgd}{psnrlvqa^{2}}\sin mjztkure d mjztkure
\]

Here \( lxvceqgd \) and \( psnrlvqa \) are functions of \( mjztkure \).
We have \( psnrlvqa\cos lxvceqgd+fjwsnoxa\cos mjztkure=ekswptzy \), so the integral becomes
\[
2\pi knsqbalv\,oaxfbrge\,fjwsnoxa^{2} ycvrmpth\int_{0}^{\pi}\frac{ekswptzy-fjwsnoxa\cos mjztkure}{psnrlvqa^{3}}\sin mjztkure d mjztkure
\]

This apparently complicated integral can be easily evaluated if we change the variable of integration to \( psnrlvqa \). By the law of cosines we have
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\qquad psnrlvqa^{2}=ekswptzy^{2}+fjwsnoxa^{2}-2 fjwsnoxa ekswptzy\cos mjztkure . \\
\text { Therefore } \\
\qquad ekswptzy-fjwsnoxa\cos mjztkure=\frac{1}{2 ekswptzy}\left(ekswptzy^{2}-fjwsnoxa^{2}+psnrlvqa^{2}\right) \\
\text { and } psnrlvqa d psnrlvqa=fjwsnoxa ekswptzy\sin mjztkure d mjztkure . \text { Hence } \\
\text { (1) } \begin{aligned}
\\
\qquad \begin{aligned}
 dsqjplui & =\frac{\pi knsqbalv\,oaxfbrge\,fjwsnoxa ycvrmpth}{ekswptzy^{2}}\int_{ekswptzy-fjwsnoxa}^{ekswptzy+fjwsnoxa}\frac{ekswptzy^{2}-fjwsnoxa^{2}+psnrlvqa^{2}}{psnrlvqa^{2}} d psnrlvqa \\
= & \frac{\pi knsqbalv\,oaxfbrge\,fjwsnoxa ycvrmpth}{ekswptzy^{2}}\left[\left(ekswptzy^{2}-fjwsnoxa^{2}\right)\left[\frac{1}{ekswptzy-fjwsnoxa}-\frac{1}{ekswptzy+fjwsnoxa}\right]+2 fjwsnoxa\right] \\
= & \frac{4\pi knsqbalv\,oaxfbrge\,fjwsnoxa^{2} ycvrmpth}{ekswptzy^{2}}=\frac{knsqbalv\,oaxfbrge\,pcjzwieh}{ekswptzy^{2}}
\end{aligned}
\end{aligned} .
\end{array}
\]
where \( pcjzwieh=4\pi fjwsnoxa^{2} ycvrmpth \) is the mass of the spherical shell. Thus the force is the same as it would be if all the mass of the shell were concentrated at the center.

Remark 1. It follows from this result that any spherically homogeneous mass distribution within a sphere \( xboqfwan \) has the same gravitational field at points outside \( xboqfwan \) as it would have if all the mass were concentrated at the center of \( xboqfwan \).
Remark 2. The same computations handle the case in which \( wsykrdjl \) is within the shell except that \( ekswptzy<fjwsnoxa \) and the limits of integration in (1) are from \( fjwsnoxa-ekswptzy \) to \( fjwsnoxa+ekswptzy \). We find then \( dsqjplui=0 \). Thus the attraction of a homogeneous spherical shell on a particle inside it is always zero.
Second Solution. Gauss's theorem states that if a closed surface \( xboqfwan \) contains a mass \( pcjzwieh \) in its interior, and if \( \vec{dsqjplui} \) is the gravitational field due to the mass, then the total flux through \( xboqfwan \) is
\[
\int_{xboqfwan} \int \vec{dsqjplui}\cdot\vec{ugevmczr} d zihtrmka=-4\pi knsqbalv\,pcjzwieh
\]
where \( \vec{ugevmczr} \) is the outward-drawn unit normal to \( xboqfwan \) and \( d zihtrmka \) is the element of surface area.
Now let \( xboqfwan \) be a sphere of radius \( ekswptzy \) external to the given shell and concentric with it. By symmetry,
\[
\vec{dsqjplui}=-dsqjplui(ekswptzy)\,\vec{ugevmczr} \text { on } xboqfwan
\]
where \( dsqjplui=dsqjplui(ekswptzy) \) is a constant on \( xboqfwan \). Thus,
\[
-4\pi knsqbalv\,pcjzwieh=\int_{xboqfwan} \int -dsqjplui(ekswptzy)\,\vec{ugevmczr}\cdot\vec{ugevmczr} d zihtrmka=-dsqjplui(ekswptzy)\int_{xboqfwan} \int d zihtrmka=-4\pi ekswptzy^{2} dsqjplui(ekswptzy)
\]
whence \( dsqjplui(ekswptzy)=knsqbalv\,pcjzwieh/ekswptzy^{2} \), and as a vector \( \vec{dsqjplui}=-\left(knsqbalv\,pcjzwieh/ekswptzy^{2}\right) \vec{ugevmczr} \).
Solution. Suppose that \( vqlenohc \) is a line through \( (rnemtqsw,ldexfoki,gpyhwazk) \) which lies in the surface \( vbedmciu=qzxwvtnp hjgrksla \). \( vqlenohc \) can be represented parametrically by
\[
\begin{array}{l}
qzxwvtnp=rnemtqsw+vdyquphs kzmhrnvu \\
hjgrksla=ldexfoki+wgznrjco kzmhrnvu \\
vbedmciu=gpyhwazk+qbvspfla kzmhrnvu
\end{array}
\]
where \( vdyquphs, wgznrjco, qbvspfla \) are not all zero. In order that \( vqlenohc \) lie in the given surface it is necessary and sufficient that
\[
 gpyhwazk+qbvspfla kzmhrnvu=(rnemtqsw+vdyquphs kzmhrnvu)(ldexfoki+wgznrjco kzmhrnvu)=rnemtqsw ldexfoki+(vdyquphs ldexfoki+wgznrjco rnemtqsw) kzmhrnvu+vdyquphs wgznrjco kzmhrnvu^{2}
\]
for all values of \( kzmhrnvu \). This implies that \( vdyquphs wgznrjco \), the coefficient of \( kzmhrnvu^{2} \), is zero; hence either \( vdyquphs=0 \) or \( wgznrjco=0 \).
If \( vdyquphs=0 \), then \( qbvspfla=wgznrjco rnemtqsw \), while if \( wgznrjco=0 \), then \( qbvspfla=vdyquphs ldexfoki \). We cannot have both \( vdyquphs \) and \( wgznrjco \) zero, since that would imply also \( qbvspfla=0 \). Hence we can normalize whichever of these is non-zero to be one. Therefore the parametric equations of \( vqlenohc \) can be put in one of the two forms
\[
\begin{array}{lll}
qzxwvtnp=rnemtqsw & qzxwvtnp & =rnemtqsw+kzmhrnvu \\
hjgrksla=ldexfoki+kzmhrnvu & \text { or } & hjgrksla=ldexfoki \\
vbedmciu=gpyhwazk+rnemtqsw kzmhrnvu & & vbedmciu=gpyhwazk+ldexfoki kzmhrnvu
\end{array}
\]
with corresponding non-parametric forms
\[
\begin{array}{lll}
qzxwvtnp=rnemtqsw & \text { or } & hjgrksla=ldexfoki \\
vbedmciu=rnemtqsw hjgrksla & & vbedmciu=ldexfoki qzxwvtnp .
\end{array}
\]

Conversely, it is obvious that any such line lies entirely on the surface.
An alternative procedure depends on the following fact: If a line \( vqlenohc \) through a point \( wsykrdjl \) lies entirely on a surface \( xboqfwan \), then \( vqlenohc \) lies in the tangent plane to \( xboqfwan \) at \( wsykrdjl \).
For the given surface
(1)
\[
vbedmciu=qzxwvtnp hjgrksla
\]
the tangent plane at \( (rnemtqsw,ldexfoki,gpyhwazk) \) is
(2)
\[
(qzxwvtnp-rnemtqsw) ldexfoki+(hjgrksla-ldexfoki) rnemtqsw+(vbedmciu-gpyhwazk)(-1)=0 .
\]

Eliminating \( vbedmciu \) between the last two equations we find
(3)
\[
(qzxwvtnp-rnemtqsw)(hjgrksla-ldexfoki)=0
\]
and this is the equation of the projection on the \( qzxwvtnp hjgrksla \)-plane of the intersection of the surface (1) with its tangent plane (2). The projection consists of two lines
and these come from two lines
```
qzxwvtnp=rnemtqsw\quad\text { and }\quad hjgrksla=ldexfoki
vbedmciu=rnemtqsw hjgrksla\quad vbedmciu
```
lying on (1) and (2).

kernel_variant.question:
Let \mathbb{R}^n, n \geq  3, be equipped with the usual Euclidean metric and let  
Sn-1(b)= {x\in \mathbb{R}^n : \|x\| = b}  
denote the (n - 1)-dimensional sphere of radius b centred at the origin O.  
A thin spherical shell is obtained by distributing mass with the constant surface-mass density \mu  (mass per unit (n - 1)-area) over Sn-1(b).  Its total mass is therefore  

  M = \mu  \cdot  \omega _{n-1} b^{n-1},  \omega _k = 2\pi ^{(k+1)/2}/\Gamma ((k+1)/2) (surface area of the unit k-sphere).

For a point P at distance \rho  = \|OP\| \neq  b answer all parts below.

(a) Show that the gravitational field g(P) produced by the shell is radial, i.e. of the form  
  g(P) = -g(\rho ) e_r, e_r = P/\|P\|.  

(b) Using the n-dimensional Gauss law for gravity deduce an explicit formula for the magnitude g(\rho ) when \rho  > b, and prove that the shell acts on external points exactly as though its entire mass M were concentrated at the centre.

(c) Show that g(\rho ) = 0 whenever \rho  < b, and conclude that the gravitational potential \Phi  is constant throughout the region bounded by the shell.

(d) Determine an explicit expression for the potential \Phi (\rho ) (choose \Phi  \to  0 as \rho  \to  \infty ) for all \rho  \neq  b, and show that \Phi  is harmonic (i.e. satisfies the n-dimensional Laplace equation \Delta \Phi  = 0) in the regions \rho  > b and \rho  < b individually.

(e) Finally, examine the limit n \to  2 formally and explain why \Phi  acquires a logarithmic behaviour analogous to the electrostatic potential of a circular ring in two dimensions.
kernel_variant.solution:
Because the argument involves several intertwined ideas we divide the proof into short lemmas, retaining the explanatory style of the classical three-dimensional treatment.

Preliminaries and notation.  For k \geq  1 let \omega _k be the surface area of the unit k-sphere in \mathbb{R}^{k+1}; explicit evaluation via the gamma-function yields the formula stated in the problem.  Throughout we write K for the universal gravitational constant in n dimensions; strictly speaking its numerical value depends on n, but this plays no role in the logical deductions that follow.

Lemma 1 (Symmetry).  
Because both the mass distribution and the underlying Euclidean metric are invariant under the full rotation group O(n), any tensorial quantity constructed from them must inherit that symmetry.  In particular, for every isometry R \in  O(n) one has R g(P)=g(RP).  Taking P on the positive x_1-axis and rotating about that axis shows that g(P) cannot have tangential components; hence

  g(P)=-g(\rho ) e_r with g(\rho )\geq 0.                                 \blacksquare 

Note that the minus sign is inserted by convention so that the field is attractive.

Lemma 2 (n-dimensional Gauss law).  
Newtonian gravity in \mathbb{R}^n is governed by the Poisson equation

  \nabla \cdot g = -S_n K \rho _m, with S_n = \omega _{n-1},

where \rho _m denotes the volume mass-density.  Integrating over a volume V and using the divergence theorem gives

  \oint _{\partial V} g\cdot n dA = -S_n K M_enc(V).                                \blacksquare 

Part (b): Field for \rho  > b.  
Choose the concentric (n - 1)-sphere S_\rho  of radius \rho  as Gaussian surface.  By Lemma 1, on S_\rho  one has g = -g(\rho ) n_out where n_out is the outward unit normal.  Hence

  \oint _{S_\rho } g\cdot n_out dA = -g(\rho ) \omega _{n-1} \rho ^{n-1}.             (1)

Since \rho  > b, S_\rho  encloses the entire shell, so M_enc = M.  Substituting (1) into Lemma 2 gives

  -g(\rho ) \omega _{n-1} \rho ^{n-1} = -S_n K M = -\omega _{n-1} K M.      (2)

Cancelling the common factor \omega _{n-1} we obtain the desired explicit formula:

  g(\rho ) = K M \rho ^{1-n}.                                           (3)

Observe that for n = 3 this reproduces the familiar inverse-square law.  Equation (3) proves that, outside the shell, the field is identical to that produced by a point mass M situated at O.

Part (c): Field for \rho  < b.  
Now let \rho  < b.  The same Gaussian surface S_\rho  encloses no mass, hence M_enc = 0.  Relation (2) becomes

  -g(\rho ) \omega _{n-1} \rho ^{n-1} = 0 \Rightarrow  g(\rho )=0.                         (4)

Since g(\rho ) vanishes, the radial derivative d\Phi /d\rho  also vanishes (by definition g = -d\Phi /d\rho ), so \Phi  is constant throughout the solid ball of radius b.  It follows that no net gravitational force acts on an interior point, extending the classical three-dimensional result.

Part (d): Explicit potential and its harmonicity.

Step 1: Exterior region \rho  > b.  
Using (3) and the relation g = -d\Phi /d\rho  we integrate outward from \rho  to \infty , imposing the boundary condition \Phi (\infty )=0:

  \Phi (\rho ) = -\int _{\rho }^{\infty } g(s) ds  
        = -\int _{\rho }^{\infty } K M s^{1-n} ds  
        = -K M [ s^{2-n}/(2-n) ]_{\rho }^{\infty }.

Because n \geq  3 the exponent 2-n is negative, whence the \infty -endpoint gives zero and

  \Phi _ext(\rho ) = K M \rho ^{2-n}/(n-2).                         (5)

Step 2: Interior region \rho  < b.  
Inside the shell g=0, so \Phi  is constant.  Its value must coincide with the limit of \Phi _ext as \rho \downarrow b to guarantee continuity of \Phi  (although the gradient may jump).  Hence

  \Phi _int(\rho ) = \Phi _int(b) = K M b^{2-n}/(n-2).            (6)

Note that the potential is continuous across \rho =b, but its derivative jumps by exactly g(b+)=K M b^{1-n}, corresponding to the surface mass density.

Step 3: Harmonicity.  
For \rho  \neq  b one has either g=0 or g given by (3).  Direct computation shows \Delta \Phi  = 0 in both domains.  Indeed, writing \Phi (\rho )=a \rho ^{2-n} with a constant a, one checks that the radial Laplacian in n dimensions,

  \Delta \Phi  = \Phi '' + (n-1)\rho ^{-1}\Phi ',  (7)

vanishes whenever a is constant and n \geq  3.  Likewise, \Phi _int is constant and therefore harmonic.  This completes part (d).

Part (e): Formal limit n \to  2.  
Equation (5) contains the factor 1/(n-2), signalling a removable singularity as n\to 2.  Expanding \rho ^{2-n}=e^{(2-n)ln\rho }\approx 1+(2-n)ln\rho , we obtain

  \Phi _ext(\rho )\approx K M [1+(2-n)ln\rho ]/(n-2)  
          = K M ln \rho  + O(n-2).

Thus in two dimensions the potential behaves like K M ln \rho , exactly the logarithmic dependence familiar from planar electrostatics or from the field of a circular mass ring of negligible thickness.  Similarly, \Phi _int approaches the constant K M ln b, preserving continuity.  Although classical Newtonian gravity is ill-defined in two spatial dimensions (since the field no longer decays at infinity), the computation illustrates how the n-dimensional formula degenerates to the logarithmic law when n = 2.

_replacement_note.reason: Original kernel variant was too easy compared to the original problem
